input.background: Recall the illustrative example on the topic of alcohol consumption and income level. Evidence of distributional asymmetry and unequal variances was demonstrated (e.g., Figure 13.17). Although ANOVA and other Normal techniques are precluded by these facts, we may still apply a Kruskal-Wallis test.
input.question: What is the p-value for the Kruskal-Wallis test in this example?

alcohol_consumption.value: Values representing alcohol consumption levels (not explicitly provided in the problem but implied as part of the dataset)
alcohol_consumption.description: Alcohol consumption levels measured for different groups in the study.

income_level.value: Values representing income levels (not explicitly provided in the problem but implied as part of the dataset)
income_level.description: Income levels used to group the observations in the study.

p_value.value: The p-value obtained from the Kruskal-Wallis test (not explicitly provided in the problem but is the target output)
p_value.description: The p-value resulting from the Kruskal-Wallis test, indicating the statistical significance of differences between groups.
